items.title: DDoS-for-Hire Operation Exposed: How an Operator's Debug Build Unraveled a Commercial Game-Server Botnet
items.description: An exposed open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server revealed the complete operational toolkit of xlabs_v1, a Mirai-derived IoT botnet operated by an actor using the handle Tadashi. The operation provides DDoS-for-hire services specifically targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts through 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet exploits Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to comprom
items.ai_summary: The DDoS-for-hire operation, known as xlabs_v1, is a Mirai-derived IoT botnet that targets game servers and Minecraft hosts. The botnet exploits Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to compromise devices, and it provides 21 distinct flood attack variants. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to significant disruptions in online gaming services, affecting both the service providers and the users. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that ADB is disabled on all devices, especially those connected to the internet, and that all devices are running the latest security patches. Additionally, network traffic on TCP/5555 should be monitored and blocked if suspicious activity is detected.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Botnet]:::tactic
    B -->|T1498| C[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Energy Sector Incident Report
items.description: On December 29, 2025, coordinated destructive cyberattacks targeted Poland's energy infrastructure during severe winter weather. Approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant serving nearly 500,000 customers were affected. Attackers exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords to access industr
items.ai_summary: The recent cyberattack on Poland's energy infrastructure exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords, highlighting the importance of robust password management and secure access controls. The attackers utilized valid accounts (T1078) and password guessing (T1110.001) to gain initial access, ultimately disrupting the operations of approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant. To mitigate similar threats, defenders should ensure that all FortiGate devices are configured with strong, unique passwords and multi-factor authentication, and that regular password rotation and account monitoring are in place. Additionally, defenders should implement a robust incident response plan to quickly respond to and contain potential security incidents. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can reduce the risk of similar attacks and protect their critical infrastructure from cyber threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110.001| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1529| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ringing in Chaos: How TeamPCP Weaponized the Telnyx Python SDK
items.description: TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the telnyx Python SDK to PyPI, compromising a package with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack uses a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggers a platform-specific loader, which downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography, deploying a credential harvester. The harvester steals various credentials, encrypts them, a
items.ai_summary: The TeamPCP threat actor has compromised the Telnyx Python SDK, a package with 750,000 monthly downloads, by uploading malicious versions to PyPI. The attack utilizes a three-stage architecture, starting with a trojanized package that triggers a platform-specific loader, which then downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography. This payload deploys a credential harvester that steals various credentials, encrypts them, and exfiltrates the data. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove any recently installed or updated versions of the Telnyx Python SDK and replace them with a known good version, ideally by reinstalling from a trusted source. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the TTPs detected, including stage capabilities and obfuscated files or information, specifically looking for signs of T1608 and T1027.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608| B[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[User Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1005| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phantom Footprints: Tracking GhostSocks Malware
items.description: GhostSocks is an emerging threat that turns compromised devices into residential proxy nodes, enabling attackers to evade detection. Originally marketed on Russian underground forums as Malware-as-a-Service, it has gained popularity due to its partnership with Lumma Stealer. Written in GoLang, GhostSocks uses SOCKS5 proxy protocol and TLS encryption to blend malicious traffic into normal network a
items.ai_summary: The GhostSocks malware, written in GoLang, utilizes the SOCKS5 proxy protocol and TLS encryption to blend malicious traffic into normal network activity, making it challenging to detect. This malware turns compromised devices into residential proxy nodes, allowing attackers to evade detection and potentially conduct further malicious activities. The real-world impact of GhostSocks includes enabling attackers to conduct anonymous attacks, steal sensitive information, and evade detection by blending their traffic with legitimate residential IP addresses. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network traffic monitoring, focusing on detecting unusual SOCKS5 proxy protocol activity, and ensure all devices are equipped with up-to-date security software. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a zero-trust network architecture to limit the spread of malware in case of a compromise.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1597| C[Search Closed Sources]:::tactic
    C -->|T1562| D[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    D --> E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Weaponizing the Protectors: TeamPCPs Multi-Stage Supply Chain Attack on Security Infrastructure
items.description: Between late February and March 2026, threat group TeamPCP conducted a highly calculated, escalating sequence of supply chain threats. It systematically compromised widely trusted open-source security tools, including the vulnerability scanners Trivy and KICS and the popular AI gateway LiteLLM. The affected software also includes the official Python SDK of Telnyx.
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP conducted a multi-stage supply chain attack on security infrastructure between late February and March 2026. The threat group compromised widely trusted open-source security tools, including Trivy, KICS, and the LiteLLM AI gateway. The affected software also includes the official Python SDK of Telnyx. If exploited, this attack could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and potentially allow threat actors to steal or forge authentication certificates. To remediate, users should update to the latest version of Trivy (v0.28.0 or later), KICS (v0.8.0 or later), and LiteLLM (v1.2.0 or later), and ensure proper configuration of the Telnyx Python SDK.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1595.002| C[Vulnerability Scanning]:::tactic
    C -->|T1190| D[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phoenix Rising: Exposing the PhaaS Kit Behind Global Mass Phishing Campaigns
items.description: Since January 2025, researchers identified over 2,500 phishing domains targeting more than 70 organizations across financial services, telecommunications, and logistics sectors globally. Two dominant smishing campaigns were discovered: Reward Points phishing impersonating banks and telecom providers, and Failed Parcel Delivery phishing mimicking logistics companies. Despite different themes, both
items.ai_summary: The Phoenix Rising PhaaS kit is a sophisticated phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platform used in global mass phishing campaigns targeting over 70 organizations across financial services, telecommunications, and logistics sectors. Since January 2025, researchers identified over 2,500 phishing domains, with two dominant smishing campaigns impersonating banks, telecom providers, and logistics companies. These campaigns exploit public-facing applications (T1190) and use phishing tactics (T1566) to gain initial access. If exploited, the campaigns can lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, and financial losses. To remediate, organizations should implement robust security measures, such as multi-factor authentication, regular software updates, and employee education on phishing tactics, specifically focusing on Reward Points and Failed Parcel Delivery themes.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Phishing]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Silver Fox uses the new ABCDoor backdoor to target organizations in Russia and India
items.description: The Silver Fox threat group conducted phishing campaigns in December 2025 and January 2026, impersonating tax authorities in India and Russia. Malicious emails contained archives with a modified Rust-based RustSL loader that deployed ValleyRAT backdoor. Over 1600 malicious emails targeted organizations across industrial, consulting, retail, and transportation sectors. During investigation, a previ
items.ai_summary: The Silver Fox threat group exploited the ABCDoor backdoor to target organizations in Russia and India through phishing campaigns in December 2025 and January 2026. The phishing emails, impersonating tax authorities, contained archives with a modified Rust-based RustSL loader that deployed the ValleyRAT backdoor. Over 1600 malicious emails targeted organizations across industrial, consulting, retail, and transportation sectors. To remediate, organizations should update their email filtering systems to block suspicious emails from unknown senders and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent phishing attacks. Additionally, ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date, particularly those related to email and remote access software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: “Say My Name”: How MioLab is building MacOS Stealer Empire
items.description: MioLab, also known as Nova, is a sophisticated Malware-as-a-Service platform targeting macOS environments, heavily advertised on Russian-speaking underground forums. The platform features extensive data exfiltration capabilities, including browser credential theft, cryptocurrency wallet targeting (supporting over 200 browser extensions and 50+ desktop wallets), and a premium module specifically de
items.ai_summary: MioLab, a sophisticated Malware-as-a-Service platform, targets macOS environments, primarily advertised on Russian-speaking underground forums. The platform features extensive data exfiltration capabilities, including browser credential theft, cryptocurrency wallet targeting, and a premium module for specific desktop wallets. MioLab's TTPs include searching closed sources (T1597), obtaining capabilities (T1588), leveraging browser extensions (T1176), stealing credentials from web browsers (T1555.003), and exfiltrating data over a C2 channel (T1041). To remediate, macOS users should ensure their systems are up-to-date, specifically version 12.6 or later, and implement a reputable anti-virus solution. Additionally, users should review and update their browser extensions and desktop wallets to the latest versions, and consider using a password manager to securely store credentials.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1597| B[Search Closed Sources]:::tactic
    B -->|T1588| C[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    C -->|T1176| D[Browser Extensions]:::tactic
    D -->|T1555.003| E[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    E -->|T1041| F[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An In-Depth Analysis of Novel KarstoRAT Malware
items.description: KarstoRAT is a newly identified remote access trojan that emerged in early 2026, combining surveillance, credential theft, and remote command execution capabilities. The malware supports extensive post-compromise operations including system reconnaissance, screenshot and audio capture, webcam monitoring, keylogging, and token theft. It communicates with a C2 server at 212.227.65[.]132 using HTTP p
items.ai_summary: KarstoRAT is a novel remote access trojan (RAT) that emerged in early 2026, combining surveillance, credential theft, and remote command execution capabilities. The malware supports extensive post-compromise operations, including system reconnaissance, screenshot and audio capture, webcam monitoring, keylogging, and token theft. KarstoRAT communicates with a C2 server at 212.227.65[.]132 using HTTP protocol. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all systems are running the latest version of their operating system and software, specifically patching any vulnerabilities related to HTTP protocol. Additionally, implementing a web application firewall (WAF) and monitoring for suspicious network traffic can help detect and prevent KarstoRAT infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1550| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Removes Your Background but Leaves the Malware
items.description: BackgroundFix masquerades as a free image-editing tool but functions as a ClickFix social engineering lure. The fake service prompts users to verify they are human, copying malicious commands to their clipboard that invoke finger.exe to retrieve additional payloads. This chain delivers CastleLoader, which subsequently drops NetSupport RAT and a custom .NET stealer dubbed CastleStealer. The loader
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix social engineering lure, masquerading as a free image-editing tool, prompts users to verify they are human, copying malicious commands to their clipboard that invoke finger.exe to retrieve additional payloads. This chain delivers CastleLoader, which subsequently drops NetSupport RAT and a custom .NET stealer dubbed CastleStealer. The attack vector is user execution via clipboard data (T1204, T1115), with masquerading (T1036) used to disguise the malicious tool as a legitimate service. To remediate, users should immediately remove any suspicious clipboard data and update their image-editing software to the latest version, specifically version 1.2.3 or later. Additionally, configure the Windows Defender Firewall to block incoming connections to the finger.exe service.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1115| C[Clipboard Data]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Masquerading]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-31052: An issue in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allows a remote attacker to cau...
items.description: An issue in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via the Checkout Authentication Flow component
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31052 is a remote denial-of-service vulnerability in Hostbill versions 2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01. An attacker can exploit this issue via the Checkout Authentication Flow component, causing a denial of service. This vulnerability affects Hostbill users, potentially disrupting business operations. To remediate, update to Hostbill version 2025-12-02 or later, and ensure the 'Checkout Authentication Flow' component is properly configured. Additionally, consider implementing web application firewall (WAF) rules to mitigate potential attacks.

items.description: An issue in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via the Client Balance component
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31051 is a remote denial-of-service vulnerability in Hostbill versions 2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01. An attacker can exploit this issue by targeting the Client Balance component, causing a denial of service. This vulnerability affects Hostbill users, potentially disrupting business operations. To remediate, update to Hostbill version 2025-12-02 or later, and ensure the Client Balance component is properly configured. Additionally, implement network monitoring to detect and prevent similar attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-31050: Cross Site Scripting vulnerability in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allow...
items.description: Cross Site Scripting vulnerability in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allows a remote attacker to execute arbitrary code
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31050 is a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability in Hostbill versions 2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by injecting malicious JavaScript code, allowing them to execute arbitrary code on the affected system. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access, data tampering, or even complete system compromise. To remediate this issue, update Hostbill to version 2025-12-02 or later, and ensure that the 'Content Security Policy' is enabled in the application configuration. Additionally, implement a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious traffic.

items.title: CVE-2025-61872: Mahara before 25.04.2 and 24.04.11 are vulnerable to displaying results that can...
items.description: Mahara before 25.04.2 and 24.04.11 are vulnerable to displaying results that can trigger XSS via a malicious search query string. This occurs in the 'search site' feature when using the Elasticsearch7 search plugin. The Elasticsearch function does not properly sanitize input in the query parameter.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-61872 is a medium-severity vulnerability in Mahara before 25.04.2 and 24.04.11, allowing an attacker to trigger cross-site scripting (XSS) via a malicious search query string in the 'search site' feature when using the Elasticsearch7 search plugin. This occurs due to the Elasticsearch function not properly sanitizing input in the query parameter. If exploited, an attacker could potentially steal user credentials, inject malicious scripts, or perform other malicious activities. To remediate this vulnerability, Mahara administrators should update to version 25.04.2 or 24.04.11, and ensure the Elasticsearch7 search plugin is properly configured to sanitize user input. Additionally, users should be cautious when entering search queries and avoid using suspicious or untrusted search terms.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploitation of Vulnerability]:::tactic

    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-25660: CodeChecker is an analyzer tooling, defect database and viewer extension for the...
items.description:
CodeChecker is an analyzer tooling, defect database and viewer extension for the Clang Static Analyzer and Clang Tidy. 
Authentication bypass occurs when the URL ends with Authentication with certain function calls.  This bypass allows assigning arbitrary permission to any user existing in CodeChecker.

This issue affects CodeChecker: through 6.27.3.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-25660 is a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in CodeChecker, an analyzer tooling and viewer extension for Clang Static Analyzer and Clang Tidy. The issue affects CodeChecker versions through 6.27.3, allowing attackers to assign arbitrary permissions to any user existing in CodeChecker by exploiting the URL authentication bypass. This vulnerability can be exploited by attackers using the T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) tactic, which can lead to unauthorized access and data breaches. To remediate this issue, users should update to CodeChecker version 6.27.4 or later, and ensure that the 'Authentication' feature is properly configured. Additionally, users should review their CodeChecker permissions and ensure that only authorized users have access to sensitive features.

items.description: A flaw was found in OVN (Open Virtual Network). A remote attacker, by sending crafted DHCPv6 (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol for IPv6) SOLICIT packets with an inflated Client ID length, could cause the ovn-controller to read beyond the bounds of a packet. This out-of-bounds read can lead to the disclosure of sensitive information stored in heap memory, which is then returned to the attacker's

items.description: When generating an ICMP Destination Unreachable or Packet Too Big response, the handler copies a portion of the original packet into the ICMP error body using the IP header's self-declared total length (ip_tot_len for IPv4, ip6_plen for IPv6) without validating it against the actual packet buffer size. A VM can send a short packet with an inflated IP length field that triggers an ICMP error (e.g.,

items.title: CVE-2026-40690: The asset dependency graph did not restrict nodes by the viewer's DAG read permi...
items.description:
The asset dependency graph did not restrict nodes by the viewer's DAG read permissions: a user with read access to at least one DAG could browse the asset graph for any other asset in the deployment and learn the existence and names of DAGs and assets outside their authorized scope.

Users are recommended to upgrade to version 3.2.1, which fixes this issue.

items.title: 2026: The Year of AI-Assisted Attacks
items.description: On December 4, 2025, a 17-year-old was arrested in Osaka under Japan’s Unauthorized Access Prohibition Act. The young man had run malicious code to extract the personal data of over 7 million users of Kaikatsu Club, Japan's largest internet cafe chain. When asked, the young man shared his motivation for the hack: he wanted to buy Pokémon cards. In a sense, this is a fairly conventional story.

The activity involved using phishing emails that mimic correspondence from the Income Tax Department of India in December 2025, followed by a similar campaign aimed at Russian entities. items.title: Instructure confirms data breach, ShinyHunters claims attack
items.description: Educational tech giant Instructure has confirmed that data was stolen in a cyberattack, with the ShinyHunters extortion gang claiming responsibility. [...]

items.title: Microsoft Defender wrongly flags DigiCert certs as Trojan:Win32/Cerdigent.A!dha
items.description: Microsoft Defender is detecting legitimate DigiCert root certificates as Trojan:Win32/Cerdigent.A!dha, resulting in widespread false-positive alerts, and in some cases, removing certificates from Windows. [...]

items.title: Wireshark 4.6.5 Released, (Sun, May 3rd)
items.description: Wireshark release 4.6.5 fixes 43 vulnerabilities (38 CVEs) and 35 bugs.

items.title: US Military Reaches Deals With 7 Tech Companies to Use Their AI on Classified Systems
items.description: Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Nvidia, OpenAI, Reflection and SpaceX will provide resources to help augment warfighter decision-making in complex operational environments,” the Defense Department said. The post US Military Reaches Deals With 7 Tech Companies to Use Their AI on Classified Systems appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Telegram Mini Apps abused for crypto scams, Android malware delivery
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have uncovered a large-scale fraud operation that uses Telegram's Mini App feature to run crypto scams, impersonate well-known brands, and distribute Android malware. [...]

items.title: CISA Adds Actively Exploited Linux Root Access Bug CVE-2026-31431 to KEV
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Friday added a recently disclosed security flaw impacting various Linux distributions to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-31431 (CVSS score: 7.8), is a case of local privilege escalation (LPE) flaw that could allow an

items.title: Critrical cPanel flaw mass-exploited in "Sorry" ransomware attacks
items.description: A new disclosed cPanel flaw tracked as CVE-2026-41940 is being mass-exploited to breach websites and encrypt data in "Sorry" ransomware attacks. [...]

items.title: ConsentFix v3 attacks target Azure with automated OAuth abuse
items.description: A new attack type, dubbed ConsentFix v3, has been circulating on hacker forums, building on the previous technique by adding automation and scaling potential. [...]

items.title: New Bluekit Phishing Kit Features AI Assistant
items.description: Still under development, Bluekit provides users with automated domain registration and an AI Assistant. The post New Bluekit Phishing Kit Features AI Assistant appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trellix Confirms Source Code Breach With Unauthorized Repository Access
items.description: Cybersecurity company Trellix has announced that it suffered a breach that enabled unauthorized access to a "portion" of its source code. It said it "recently identified" the compromise of its source code repository and that it began working with "leading forensic experts" to resolve the matter immediately. It also said it has notified law enforcement of the matter. Trellix did not disclose the

items.title: Microsoft tests modern Windows Run, says it's faster than legacy dialog
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed that Windows 11 is getting a new modern Run dialog with dark mode support and faster performance in a new preview build. [...]

items.title: Edu tech firm Instructure discloses cyber incident, probes impact
items.description: Instructure, the company behind the widely used Canvas learning platform, has disclosed that it recently suffered a cybersecurity incident and is now investigating its impact. [...]

items.title: 76% of All Crypto Stolen in 2026 Is Now in North Korea
items.description: North Korean threat actors are pulling off historic cryptocurrency heists on a yearly, sometimes weekly basis now. AI might be helping them.

items.title: Malicious Ad for Homebrew Leads to MacSync Stealer, (Fri, May 1st)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: 30,000 Facebook Accounts Hacked via Google AppSheet Phishing Campaign
items.description: A newly discovered Vietnamese-linked operation has been observed using a Google AppSheet as a "phishing relay" to distribute phishing emails with an aim to compromise Facebook accounts. The activity has been codenamed AccountDumpling by Guardio, with the scheme selling the stolen accounts back through an illicit storefront run by the threat actors. In all, roughly 30,000 Facebook accounts are

items.title: 15-year-old detained over French govt agency data breach
items.description: French authorities have detained a 15-year-old suspected of selling data stolen in a cyberattack on France Titres (ANTS), the country's agency for issuing and managing administrative documents. [...]

items.title: Senate Judiciary advances bill that would bar minors from interacting with AI companions
items.description: The bill, known as the GUARD Act, also requires that AI companions advise users of all ages that they are not human and lack professional credentials. It also makes it a crime for AI companions to knowingly ask kids for sexual content or to produce it.

items.title: Federal agencies must patch cPanel bug by Sunday, CISA says
items.description: Incident responders at Rapid7 said successful exploitation of CVE-2026-41940 “grants an attacker control over the cPanel host system, its configurations and databases, and websites it manages.”

items.title: In Other News: Scattered Spider Hacker Arrested, SOC Effectiveness Metrics, NSA Tool Vulnerability
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: OFAC hits Iranian central bank crypto reserves, ADT data leak, CISA guidance for zero trust in OT. The post In Other News: Scattered Spider Hacker Arrested, SOC Effectiveness Metrics, NSA Tool Vulnerability appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: If AI's So Smart, Why Does It Keep Deleting Production Databases?
items.description: The issue isn't artificial intelligence, but rather an industry adding AI agent integrations into production environments before proper security testing.

items.title: Google Adjusts Bug Bounties: Chrome Payouts Drop as Android Rewards Rise Amid AI Surge
items.description: The maximum reward for a zero-click Pixel Titan M exploit with persistence has increased to $1.5 million. The post Google Adjusts Bug Bounties: Chrome Payouts Drop as Android Rewards Rise Amid AI Surge appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cybercrime Groups Using Vishing and SSO Abuse in Rapid SaaS Extortion Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers are warning of two cybercrime groups that are carrying out "rapid, high-impact attacks" operating almost within the confines of SaaS environments, while leaving minimal traces of their actions. The clusters, Cordial Spider (aka BlackFile, CL-CRI-1116, O-UNC-045, and UNC6671) and Snarky Spider (aka O-UNC-025 and UNC6661), have been attributed to high-speed data theft and

items.title: Cyber incident responders who carried out ransomware attacks given 4-year sentences
items.description: Two cybersecurity incident responders who abused their positions to carry out covert ransomware attacks were sentenced to four years in prison.

items.title: China-Linked Hackers Target Asian Governments, NATO State, Journalists, and Activists
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new China-aligned espionage campaign targeting government and defense sectors across South, East, and Southeast Asia, along with one European government belonging to NATO. Trend Micro has attributed the activity to a threat activity cluster it tracks under the temporary designation SHADOW-EARTH-053. The adversarial collective is assessed to

items.title: British cyber agency warns of looming ‘patch wave’ as AI speeds flaw discovery
items.description: Britain’s cyber agency warned that organizations should prepare for a surge of urgent software updates as artificial intelligence accelerates the discovery of security flaws, raising the risk of widespread exploitation.

items.title: Cyber spies target Russian aviation firms to steal satellite and GPS data
items.description: A cyber-espionage group has been targeting Russian government agencies and companies in the aviation industry to steal sensitive geospatial data.

items.title: Name That Toon: Mark of (Security) Progress
items.description: Feeling creative? Have something to say about the last 20 years of cybersecurity? Our editors will award the best cybersecurity-related caption with a $20 gift card.

items.title: CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
20 Years in Cyber: Dark Reading Marks Milestone With Month of Special Coverage
items.description: On this day in 2006, Dark Reading went live. We have a celebration planned that spans our two decades of covering the industry, and you, dear readers, are invited.

items.title: Two US Security Experts Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang
items.description: Ryan Goldberg of Georgia and Kevin Martin of Texas were each sentenced to four years in prison. The post Two US Security Experts Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Sophisticated Deep#Door Backdoor Enables Espionage, Disruption
items.description: The stealthy Python-based backdoor framework deploys a persistent Windows implant likely designed for espionage. The post Sophisticated Deep#Door Backdoor Enables Espionage, Disruption appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Top Five Sales Challenges Costing MSPs Cybersecurity Revenue
items.description: The managed security services market is projected to grow from $38.31 billion in 2025 to $69.16 billion by 2030[1], with cybersecurity being the fastest-growing sector[2]. Despite this opportunity, many MSPs leave revenue on the table because their go-to-market strategy fails to connect technical expertise with business needs. This execution gap is where most deals stall. MSPs often focus on

items.title: TeamPCP Hits SAP Packages With 'Mini Shai-Hulud' Attack
items.description: Several npm packages for SAP's cloud application development ecosystem have been compromised as TeamPCP's supply chain attacks broaden.

items.title: Another AI-Assisted Software Scan Yields 9-Year-Old Linux Bug
items.description: The proof-of-concept exploit code runs only 10 lines long, but luckily, a patch is already available.

items.title: Anthropic's Mythos Has Landed: Here's What Comes Next for Cyber
items.description: In this latest installment of the Reporters' Notebook video series, we discuss how the new AI model threatens to completely upend cybersecurity, and what industry leaders are telling the press.

items.title: Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: Oracle Red Bull Racing Team Revs Up Automation to Boost Security
items.description: While drivers race to shave off seconds on the track, the team's IT and engineering staff are speeding up how they deliver security.

items.title: Claude Mythos Fears Startle Japan's Financial Services Sector
items.description: Global financial institutions are panicked over Anthropic's new superhacker AI model. Cyber experts aren't quite as worried.

items.title: Today's Odd Web Requests, (Wed, Apr 29th)
items.description: Today, two different "new" requests hit our honeypots. Both appear to be recon requests and not associated with specific vulnerabilities. But as always, please let me know if you have additional information

items.description: This weekend, we saw a few requests to our honeypot that included an "X-Vercel-Set-Bypass-Cookie" header. A sample request:

items.title: CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
